alsoKnownAs: Halloween Hermit (locally — not the Hawaiian Ciliopagurus strigatus)

nativeRange.habitat: Low intertidal and shallow sub-tidal rocky reefs along the mainland Eastern Pacific coast of Mexico, generally on rock and coral-rock structures from the central Sea of Cortez south to Acapulco; found in loose groups inhabiting empty gastropod shells.
nativeRange.biotope: Rocky tropical Eastern Pacific reef and intertidal

decorPreferences: live rock with crevices and rocky rubble for scavenging
an assortment of empty gastropod shells in graduated sizes so the crab can trade up as it grows
mature reef substrate with film algae, cyanobacteria, and detritus to graze
tightly-fitting lid is generally advised — hermits are known to wander and can leave open-top tanks

diet.notes: Generally considered an easy-to-feed scavenger. Documented eating film algae, cyanobacteria, and detritus in the wild and readily takes most prepared and frozen foods in captivity — mysis, brine shrimp, finely chopped seafood, nori sheets, and sinking pellets are commonly accepted. In a well-stocked cleanup-crew tank supplemental feeding is rarely required; in sparser systems direct feeding once or twice a week is commonly advised.

goodWith: other small reef-safe hermit crabs (e.g. blue-leg, scarlet reef)
reef-safe snails (provided spare shells are offered)
LPS, SPS, soft corals, and zoanthids
avoidWith: predatory wrasses, hawkfish, large pufferfish and triggerfish that hunt hermits
large predatory crabs and mantis shrimp
any system recently treated with copper-based medications
tanks without spare shells available — hermits have been known to kill snails for their shells when crowded

imageCandidates.notes: Photographer Don Loarie (also on iNaturalist, obs photo 581097). 1024x833. Sharp, well-lit whole-animal shot showing the diagnostic vivid red walking legs and dark brown/black chelipeds with red tips on a live individual in its gastropod shell.

summary: The Mexican Red Leg Hermit Crab (Calcinus californiensis) is a small Eastern Pacific hermit crab with vivid red walking legs, native to the Gulf of California and Mexican Pacific coast. It is available from specialty marine retailers as a cleanup-crew grazer of film algae, cyanobacteria, and detritus.
careNotes: The Mexican Red Leg Hermit Crab is stocked by specialty marine retailers as a reef-safe member of the cleanup crew, where it grazes on film algae, cyanobacteria, and detritus. The species is often confused in the trade with the Caribbean Red Reef Hermit (Paguristes cadenati) — both share trade names like 'Red Leg Hermit', but they are distinct species from different oceans; C. californiensis is the Eastern Pacific species. Note that 'Halloween Hermit Crab' more commonly refers to the Hawaiian Ciliopagurus strigatus — local Mexican use of that name for C. californiensis can also cause confusion. Generally considered reef-safe, but isolated reports exist of large or underfed individuals nipping at small polyps or harassing snails for their shells; keeping a steady food supply and offering a generous assortment of empty gastropod shells (graduated sizes, with a few larger than the crab currently uses) sharply reduces the risk. Like all marine invertebrates it is highly intolerant of copper-based medications. Drip-acclimate slowly to avoid salinity/temperature shock. Tank minimums in the trade are commonly stated at around 38 L (10 US gal), but a minimum of 75 L is a more practical floor for a small group, allowing adequate territory, shell turnover, and stable reef parameters. Captive lifespan is widely reported as only 1–2 years even though wild individuals are thought to live much longer.

primary.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
primary.notes: Used as primary because the manifest-supplied LiveAquaria URL (/product/2036/mexican-red-leg-hermit-crab) 404'd and LiveAquaria's catalog returned no Mexican Red Leg Hermit listing on search. BlueZoo's Red Leg Reef Hermit (Calcinus spp., Central America/Mexico origin) is the closest active retail listing for this species and supplied size (~1 inch), tank minimum (~10 gal), temperament (peaceful), reef-safe (yes), care level (beginner), and diet.

additional.name: Mexican Crabs (mexican-crabs.org)
additional.notes: Primary biology source for the species itself. Confirmed scientific name Calcinus californiensis (Bouvier, 1898), Eastern Pacific distribution (central Sea of Cortez to Acapulco), depth 0–6 m, adult shield 1.2–1.9 cm, omnivore diet, nocturnal activity, captive lifespan only 1–2 years (vs much longer in the wild), and frequent molting.

additional.name: Reeflex
additional.notes: Designated secondary source (Reef2Reef Wiki returned 403). Confirmed family Calcinidae and order Decapoda, Eastern Pacific range (Mexico, El Salvador), adult size up to 2 cm, and stated target temperature ~26°C. Noted that documented captive husbandry is sparse — much of the aquarium guidance is extrapolated from standard tropical reef hermit parameters.

additional.name: Récif'AtHome
additional.notes: European reef trade page (out of stock at access time). Confirmed common name 'Mexican Red Leg Hermit Crab' for C. californiensis and its role as an easy-to-maintain detritivore in cleanup-crew packs.

additional.name: Reefapp (Calcinus laevimanus reference)
additional.notes: Used only as a genus-level husbandry baseline (no C. californiensis page exists). Confirmed Calcinus hermits as 'always reef safe' and gave a 100 L floor for the larger Dwarf Zebra Hermit — informed the cautious 75 L floor written here for the smaller C. californiensis.

crustacean.moltingNotes: Reported molting frequency varies widely — from roughly monthly to as long as every 18 months — with smaller juveniles molting more often than adults. Shed exoskeletons should generally be left in the tank as a source of chitin and calcium; the crab will usually consume them. Stable salinity, calcium, and trace-element (especially iodine) levels are commonly considered supportive of successful molts.
crustacean.breedingNotes: No documented captive breeding in the hobby. Larvae are planktonic and require specialized rearing systems with appropriate microalgae and zooplankton feeds; captive propagation is not realistically achievable in a home setup.
